probe_designs.content: Compute the positive-class prevalence for each of the 40 CelebA attributes on the train/val/test splits. CelebA is known to be highly imbalanced (e.g., Bald ~2%, Mustache ~4%, No_Beard ~83%, Young ~78%). Check: (1) per-attribute positive rate distribution, (2) consistency of prevalence across splits, (3) whether the model's BCEWithLogitsLoss uses pos_weight or class-balanced weighting. Healthy signal: loss weighting or sampling compensates for imbalance; per-attribute AP/F1 reported rather than accuracy. Unhealthy: accuracy-only metric reporting that hides trivial majority-class prediction for rare attributes (e.g., 98% acc on Bald by always predicting 0).
possible_sources: Liu et al. 2015 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (CelebA)
Rudd et al. 2016 'MOON: Mixed Objective Optimization Network'
He et al. 2017 'Learning from Imbalanced Data'

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-Attribute F1, AP, and Balanced Accuracy Breakdown
probe_designs.content: Rather than reporting only mean accuracy across 40 attributes (the traditional CelebA headline metric, which is misleading under imbalance), compute per-attribute and mean F1, Average Precision (AP/AUPRC), AUROC, and balanced accuracy. Healthy: mean AP > 0.6 and no attribute collapses to AP near positive-prevalence (indicating random ranking). Unhealthy: rare attributes (Bald, Mustache, Pale_Skin, Gray_Hair) show AP near prevalence baseline while reported accuracy still looks ~98%.
possible_sources: Hand & Chellappa 2017 'Attributes for Improved Attributes' (AFFACT)
Günther et al. 2017 'AFFACT - Alignment-Free Facial Attribute Classification Technique'
He et al. 2018 'Harnessing Synthesized Abstraction Images' (PS-MCNN)

probe_designs.probe_name: Demographic Parity & Equalized Odds Across Gender/Skin-Tone Subgroups
probe_designs.content: Use the 'Male' and 'Pale_Skin' attributes (and optionally external annotations such as FairFace or CelebSET) as sensitive group proxies. Evaluate per-subgroup TPR/FPR/AP for each of the other 39 attributes. Healthy: demographic parity difference and equalized odds gap small (<0.05) for non-sex-correlated attributes. Unhealthy: large disparities — e.g., 'Attractive' or 'Young' showing higher positive rate for females, 'Wearing_Lipstick'/'Heavy_Makeup' nearly perfect on one subgroup but degraded on another, indicating the model encodes stereotyped co-occurrence.
possible_sources: Buolamwini & Gebru 2018 'Gender Shades'
Hardt et al. 2016 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning'
Ramaswamy et al. 2021 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing'
Denton et al. 2019 'Image Counterfactual Sensitivity Analysis'

probe_designs.content: Because the model outputs independent sigmoids for 40 attributes, calibration must be assessed per head. Bin predicted probabilities into 10–15 bins and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Brier score per attribute. Plot reliability diagrams. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 per attribute; diagonal reliability curves. Unhealthy: systematic overconfidence (common with BCE + modern deep nets) especially on imbalanced heads; temperature scaling should be fit on val and its effect measured.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 2017 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks'
Naeini et al. 2015 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities Using Bayesian Binning'
Kull et al. 2019 'Beyond Temperature Scaling'

probe_designs.content: Track per-epoch training loss, validation loss, per-attribute val AP, and the train-val gap. Inspect at which epoch val metric plateaus or inflects while train loss continues to drop (classic overfitting). Also evaluate cross-dataset generalization by testing the CelebA-trained model on LFWA (Labeled Faces in the Wild with attributes) which shares many of the 40 attributes. Healthy: small train-val gap (<5% AP), early-stopping applied, modest domain drop on LFWA. Unhealthy: monotonically growing gap, >20% AP drop on LFWA indicating CelebA-specific shortcut features.
possible_sources: Liu et al. 2015 CelebA paper (LFWA cross-eval)
Kumar et al. 2009 'Attribute and Simile Classifiers for Face Verification'
Recht et al. 2019 'Do ImageNet Classifiers Generalize to ImageNet?'

probe_designs.probe_name: Robustness to Common Corruptions, Pose, and Occlusion
probe_designs.content: Evaluate the model under realistic perturbations: Gaussian noise, blur, JPEG compression, brightness/contrast shifts (ImageNet-C style), aligned vs. unaligned inputs, face occlusions (sunglasses/mask overlays), and head-pose variation. Measure relative mAP drop. Healthy: <10% relative mAP drop for mild severities; attributes that depend on occluded regions (e.g., Mustache under mask) degrade gracefully. Unhealthy: large drops on simple photometric changes indicate reliance on low-level statistics rather than facial semantics; alignment-sensitive drops indicate over-dependence on CelebA's aligned crops.
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich 2019 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions'
Günther et al. 2017 AFFACT (alignment robustness)
Karkkainen & Joo 2021 'FairFace'

probe_designs.content: Compute the 40×40 label co-occurrence matrix on train data and compare it to the 40×40 prediction co-occurrence matrix on test. Identify attribute pairs with highly correlated labels (e.g., Heavy_Makeup↔Wearing_Lipstick↔Male=0; Goatee↔Male=1) and check if the model over-relies on these shortcuts by counterfactually flipping one attribute's pixel evidence (e.g., remove lipstick) and measuring whether correlated attribute predictions also flip. Healthy: prediction correlations track label correlations but counterfactual edits change only the intended attribute. Unhealthy: predictions inherit stronger correlations than ground truth, and counterfactuals leak across attributes — sign of shortcut learning.
possible_sources: Denton et al. 2019 'Image Counterfactual Sensitivity Analysis for GAN-trained Classifiers'
Ramaswamy et al. 2021 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing'
Geirhos et al. 2020 'Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks'

probe_designs.content: For each of the 40 attribute heads, produce Grad-CAM or Integrated Gradients heatmaps on a sample of correctly-predicted and incorrectly-predicted faces. Qualitatively and quantitatively verify that local attributes attend to the correct face region (e.g., Eyeglasses→eye region, Goatee→chin, Wearing_Hat→top-of-head, Arched_Eyebrows→brow). Compute a pointing-game score against face-landmark-derived ROI masks. Healthy: high pointing-game accuracy (>70%) for spatially-localized attributes. Unhealthy: attention on background, clothing, or the 'Male' proxy region regardless of attribute — indicating the model uses gender as a shortcut.
possible_sources: Selvaraju et al. 2017 'Grad-CAM'
Sundararajan et al. 2017 'Integrated Gradients'
Zhang et al. 2018 'Top-Down Neural Attention by Excitation Backprop' (pointing game)
Kalayeh et al. 2017 'Improving Facial Attribute Prediction using Semantic Segmentation'

probe_designs.content: CelebA attribute labels are well-known to be noisy and subjective (e.g., Attractive, Young, Big_Nose, Oval_Face, Pointy_Nose, Pale_Skin). Apply confident-learning / Cleanlab to identify likely mislabeled examples using the trained model's predicted probabilities and observed labels. Report estimated noise rate per attribute and measure how much val AP changes when training on a cleaned subset. Healthy: subjective attributes have elevated estimated noise (10–30%) consistent with human-annotator disagreement studies; objective attributes (Eyeglasses, Wearing_Hat) have <5% estimated noise. Unhealthy: high estimated noise on objective attributes signals data-loading or label-alignment bugs.
possible_sources: Northcutt et al. 2021 'Confident Learning: Estimating Uncertainty in Dataset Labels'
Liu et al. 2015 CelebA dataset card
Lingenfelter et al. 2022 'A Quantitative Analysis of Labeling Issues in the CelebA Dataset'

probe_designs.probe_name: Backbone Capacity vs. Compute Trade-off & Head Redundancy
probe_designs.content: Profile the chosen timm backbone for params, FLOPs, peak activation memory, and throughput at 224×224. Compare per-attribute AP achieved versus a lightweight baseline (MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0) and a stronger baseline (ConvNeXt/ViT) under matched training budget. Additionally, inspect the 40-way classification head for weight redundancy via CKA/cosine similarity between per-attribute weight vectors — highly correlated heads (e.g., Wearing_Lipstick vs. Heavy_Makeup) may indicate the backbone, not the head, is the bottleneck. Healthy: AP scales monotonically with backbone capacity until diminishing returns; head similarity matches label co-occurrence structure. Unhealthy: bigger backbone yields no gain (underfitting or optimization issue) or head vectors collapse into a near-rank-1 subspace (representation collapse).
possible_sources: Wightman 2019 'PyTorch Image Models (timm)'
Tan & Le 2019 'EfficientNet'
Kornblith et al. 2019 'Similarity of Neural Network Representations Revisited (CKA)'
Dosovitskiy et al. 2020 'An Image is Worth 16×16 Words (ViT)'
